shrines.oneLine: Rauru's Blessing 'West Necluda Sky Crystal'. Move the crystal across the islands to the shrine's foundation.

shrines.name: Kumamayn Shrine
shrines.location: South Necluda Sky Archipelago (2856, -2857, 1212)
shrines.oneLine: Rauru's Blessing 'Necluda Sky Crystal'. Escort the green crystal across the floating islands to the shrine pedestal.

sets.name: Archaic / Well-Worn
sets.pieces: Archaic Tunic, Archaic Legwear, Well-Worn Trousers (no headpiece)
sets.where: Starting gear on the Great Sky Island; from chests and the Steward Construct as you begin the game.
sets.bonus: No set bonus; just basic starter defense to get you off the tutorial island.
sets.upgrade: Not upgradable at Great Fairies; starter clothing you replace fast.

sets.name: Hylian Set
sets.pieces: Hylian Hood, Hylian Tunic, Hylian Trousers
sets.where: Buy from Hateno Village's Ventest Clothing Boutique and other early shops.
sets.bonus: No set bonus, but cheap, balanced all-purpose defense for the early game.
sets.upgrade: Upgrade theme: common monster parts like Bokoblin Horns plus Hylian plants.

sets.name: Climbing Gear (Climber's Set)
sets.pieces: Climber's Bandanna, Climbing Gear (chest), Climbing Boots
sets.where: Three separate chests in caves: North Hyrule Plain Cave, Ploymus Mountain Cave, and Upland Zorana Byroad.
sets.bonus: Full set bonus Climbing Jump Stamina Up: halves the stamina used when jumping mid-climb. No per-piece speed boost.
sets.upgrade: Upgrade theme: lizards and climbing critters like Lizalfos Tails.

sets.name: Glide Set
sets.pieces: Glide Mask, Glide Shirt, Glide Tights
sets.where: Complete the three Sky dive ring challenges on Courage, Bravery, and Valor Islands (one piece each).
sets.bonus: Each piece boosts dive speed; full set upgraded to 2 stars gives Impact Proof - immunity to all fall damage.
sets.upgrade: Upgrade theme: sky/flight critters and Zonai materials.
sets.priority: Fun and very useful for fliers

sets.name: Zonaite Set
sets.pieces: Zonaite Helm, Zonaite Waistguard, Zonaite Greaves
sets.where: Three chests on the Sky Islands, in different areas of the sky.
sets.bonus: Full set bonus Energy Recharge Up: doubles how fast your Zonai battery (energy cells) recharge.
sets.upgrade: Upgrade theme: Zonaite ore and Zonai device/construct materials.

sets.name: Charged Set
sets.pieces: Charged Headdress, Charged Shirt, Charged Trousers
sets.where: Three caves along the Dracozu River (marked by Tall Dragon Pillars) during the Secret of the Ring Ruins quest.
sets.bonus: Full set bonus Stormy Weather Charge: charges weapon attacks faster while in stormy weather.
sets.upgrade: Upgrade theme: electric monster parts (Electric Lizalfos, Electric Keese).

sets.name: Froggy Set
sets.pieces: Froggy Hood, Froggy Sleeve, Froggy Leggings
sets.where: Rewards from completing all of Penn's Lucky Clover Gazette side quests (12 stable jobs) for Traysi.
sets.bonus: Slip Resistance per piece; full set greatly reduces slipping while climbing wet or icy surfaces.
sets.upgrade: Upgrade theme: frogs like Hot-Footed Frog and Tireless Frog.

sets.name: Rubber Set
sets.pieces: Rubber Helm, Rubber Armor, Rubber Tights
sets.where: Three chests in caves around Hyrule; the helm is an easy early grab before facing Gerudo storms.
sets.bonus: Shock Resistance per piece; fully upgraded set bonus Unshockable negates electric/lightning shock damage.
sets.upgrade: Upgrade theme: electric parts like Electric Lizalfos Tails.

sets.name: Desert Voe Set
sets.pieces: Desert Voe Headband, Desert Voe Spaulder, Desert Voe Trousers
sets.where: Buy from the clothing shop in Gerudo Town once you reach the desert.
sets.bonus: Heat Resistance per piece; fully upgraded set bonus adds Shock Damage Resist, ideal for the Gerudo region.
sets.upgrade: Upgrade theme: desert critters and Electric Safflina.

sets.name: Snowquill Set
sets.pieces: Snowquill Headdress, Snowquill Tunic, Snowquill Trousers
sets.where: Buy from the Rito Village shop (Brazen Beak) in the snowy Hebra/Tabantha region.
sets.bonus: Cold Resistance per piece; fully upgraded set bonus Unfreezable keeps you from freezing in blizzards.
sets.upgrade: Upgrade theme: cold-region critters (Cold Darner, Winterwing Butterfly).

sets.name: Flamebreaker Set
sets.pieces: Flamebreaker Helm, Flamebreaker Armor, Flamebreaker Boots
sets.where: Buy at the Goron armor shop in Goron City near Death Mountain.
sets.bonus: Fire Resistance per piece; fully upgraded set bonus Fireproof stops flames from burning you.
sets.upgrade: Upgrade theme: fire materials like Fireproof Lizards and Flint.

sets.name: Zora Set (Zora Armor)
sets.pieces: Zora Helm, Zora Armor, Zora Greaves
sets.where: Zora Armor from Lady Yona during Sidon of the Zora (needs an ancient arowana; Dento points you to it). Helm and greaves found around the region.
sets.bonus: Swim Speed Up per piece; full set lets you swim up waterfalls, very handy in Zora areas.
sets.upgrade: Upgrade theme: fish and Zora-region materials.

sets.name: Champion's Leathers
sets.where: Hidden chest in the Sanctum (throne room) of Hyrule Castle; light the two torches behind the throne to open the tomb.
sets.bonus: No set bonus (single piece), but strong scaling defense as you upgrade and a classic blue look.
sets.upgrade: Upgrade theme: Star Fragments plus rarer monster parts at higher stars.

sets.name: Miner's Set (glow set)
sets.pieces: Miner's Mask, Miner's Top, Miner's Trousers
sets.where: Three chests in Depths mines: Abandoned Kara Kara, Daphnes Canyon, and Hylia Canyon mines.
sets.bonus: Pieces glow to light the dark Depths; full upgraded set bonus Shining Steps leaves a glowing petal trail.
sets.upgrade: Upgrade theme: Brightcaps and luminous Depths materials.
sets.priority: Best early light source for the Depths

sets.name: Tunic of the Wild (Of the Wild Set)
sets.pieces: Cap of the Wild, Tunic of the Wild, Trousers of the Wild
sets.where: Misko's Treasure side quests - three Dark Skeletons in the Depths (Hebra, Gerudo, Eldin). The Eldin piece needs fire protection. NOT an all-shrines reward like BotW.
sets.bonus: Full set upgraded to 2 stars grants Attack Up. The classic green Champion look.
sets.upgrade: Upgrade theme: Dragon Parts (scales, claws, horns, fangs).

sets.name: Tunic of Memories
sets.pieces: Single body piece (not part of a set)
sets.where: Buy for 400 Poes from any Bargainer Statue after speaking to all of them and clearing A Call from the Depths.
sets.bonus: No set bonus; a nostalgic outfit referencing Link's classic green tunic, mainly for the look.
sets.upgrade: Upgrade theme: Depths/Gloom-adjacent materials at the Great Fairies.

sets.name: Fierce Deity Set
sets.pieces: Fierce Deity Mask, Fierce Deity Armor, Fierce Deity Boots
sets.where: Three caves (Skull Lake Cave, Citadel Ruins Summit Cave, Ancient Tree Stump) without amiibo, or via the Majora's Mask Link amiibo.
sets.bonus: Attack Up per piece; full set upgraded to 3 stars adds the Charge Atk. Stamina Up bonus. Strong offensive set.
sets.upgrade: Upgrade theme: rare monster horns/guts at higher stars.

enemies.name: Bokoblin
enemies.tactic: Sneak up for a stealth strike, or flurry-rush their swing. Fuse a horn or rock to your weapon for far higher damage on early ones.
enemies.drops: Bokoblin Horn, Bokoblin Fang, Bokoblin Guts

enemies.name: Moblin
enemies.tactic: Big and slow. Dodge the wide club swing for a flurry rush, or knock it off ledges. Fused horns/rocks make short work of it.
enemies.drops: Moblin Horn, Moblin Fang, Moblin Guts

enemies.name: Lizalfos
enemies.tactic: Fast and dodgy; they spit elemental bursts. Hit with the opposite element or just flurry-rush their lunge, then finish quickly.
enemies.drops: Lizalfos Horn, Lizalfos Tail, Lizalfos Talon

enemies.name: Horriblin
enemies.tactic: Clings to cave ceilings and grabs you with its tongue. Shoot the head to drop it, then beat it on the ground. Higher tiers spit gloom.
enemies.drops: Horriblin Horn, Horriblin Claw, Horriblin Guts

enemies.name: Aerocuda
enemies.tactic: Flying bat-eye that swoops and can carry off Bokoblins. Shoot it down with any arrow; a Keese Eyeball-fused arrow homes right in.
enemies.drops: Aerocuda Eyeball, Aerocuda Wing

enemies.name: Construct (Soldier / Captain)
enemies.tactic: Zonai automata in the Sky. Soldiers are basic; Captains hit harder. Flurry-rush, or knock their weapon off to steal a Construct weapon to Fuse.
enemies.drops: Soldier/Captain Construct Horn, Zonai Charge, Construct weapons

enemies.name: Like Like
enemies.tactic: Tube that swallows you and spits you out, dropping a weapon. Shoot the open mouth or its eye to stun, then attack. Watch for Ice/Rock variants.
enemies.drops: Opal, chest gear; Ice Like Stone or Rock Like Stone from variants

enemies.name: Gibdo
enemies.tactic: Gerudo desert mummies that harden and revive unless finished with an element. Hit with fire/electric/ice to weaken, then strike to end them.
enemies.drops: Gibdo Bone, Gibdo Wing, Gibdo Guts

enemies.name: Gloom Hands (Gloom Spawn)
enemies.tactic: Crawling gloom arms that drain max hearts and regrow if split up. Get on high ground they can't climb and rain Bomb Flower / Fire Fruit arrows into the whole cluster at once; after they die, Phantom Ganon spawns.
enemies.drops: Dark Clump
enemies.battle: Bring a strong bow with a big stack of arrows and fuse explosives to them — Bomb Flowers or Fire Fruit hit the whole cluster of arms at once — and pack a meal that restores gloom-damaged hearts (a Sundelion/"sunny" dish) or a Dark Clump warding dish, or wear Depths armor, to blunt the gloom. The fight opens with five gloom-soaked arms crawling at you, each with its own health bar; do NOT fight them on the ground, because touching them drains your max hearts, and don't pick them off one at a time — kill them together or the survivors regrow. Get up high right away: a boulder, a fallen log, or a quick Ultrahand platform works, since the hands can't climb steep verticals (they can still scramble up slopes). Stay elevated, keep your distance, and rain explosive arrows into the bunched-up cluster. If you're struggling, freeze each arm with an Ice Fruit and smash it while frozen, or simply wait them out from somewhere they can't reach. After the hands die, Phantom Ganon spawns as a second phase (sometimes more than one) — circle him and block or parry his swings; every hit you land shrinks his gloom pool until he falls. They drop Dark Clumps, which cook into warding dishes that protect against gloom.

enemies.name: Phantom Ganon
enemies.tactic: Spawns after you clear the Gloom Hands. Wear the Depths (gloom-resist) set and pack a Sundelion dish; dodge his telegraphed swing/charge for a flurry rush (or perfect-guard), punish, then step out of the heart-draining gloom pool. Avoid the spin attack — it cuts through blocks.
enemies.drops: Demon King's Bow, a Gloom weapon (Sword/Club/Spear), Dark Clump
enemies.battle: Gloom resistance wins half this fight: wear the Depths armor set (the "gloomy" gear, gloom-attack resistance) and bring a Sundelion dish (e.g. Sunny meat/mushrooms) to heal back any max-hearts the gloom locks away. Carry a strong Fused melee weapon (the Master Sword is ideal here — it does extra damage to Ganon — or a Lynel horn on a good base), a shield, and a bow with plenty of arrows. He only spawns after you clear the Gloom Hands, so handle those first: fuse bombs or explosives to your arrows, and if it gets ugly, climb onto high ground the hands can't reach and pelt them safely. Once Phantom Ganon appears, don't hug him — the gloom pool at his feet drains hearts. Wait for his telegraphed swing or charge, then backflip or side-hop at the last instant for a flurry rush, or raise your shield for a perfect guard; land a couple of hits and step back out of the pool. His spin attack cuts through blocks, so dodge that one. The pool shrinks every hit you land, so it doubles as a progress bar. If you're overwhelmed, just flee and he'll vanish. He always drops the Demon King's Bow — its damage scales with your max hearts — plus a Gloom Sword, Club, or Spear matching the one he wielded, and Dark Clumps.

enemies.name: Boss Bokoblin
enemies.tactic: Huge Bokoblin that leads camps and hurls rocks and minions. Sneakstrike or dodge its overhead for a flurry rush, hit the head, and use a heavy two-hander Fused with a rock or Boss Bokoblin Horn.
enemies.drops: Boss Bokoblin Horn, Boss Bokoblin Guts
enemies.battle: A Boss Bokoblin is a big, tanky brute that leads camps, so bring a two-handed weapon (a claymore Fused with a sturdy rock or a Boss Bokoblin Horn hits hard, and its heavy final swing knocks the brute back), a bow with arrows, and a meal that boosts attack or defense; a good shield lets you parry, and a stealth armor set sets up a huge opening. Open from stealth if you can: creep up and sneakstrike for a roughly 8x hit that can stagger or nearly drop it before the camp reacts, and taking the boss out first often scatters the smaller Bokoblins. In a straight fight, swing your two-hander to stagger and interrupt it, then bait its big overhead and dodge at the last instant for a flurry rush and a string of free hits; raise your shield to parry instead if dodging is tricky. It likes to hurl rocks, barrels, and even its own minions at you, so sidestep them or use Recall to fling the thrown object right back into its face. If it is wearing you down, hang back and pelt it with arrows, lob bomb-fused arrows, or let Tulin's gust and your companions chip it while you stay out of reach. The base Boss Bokoblin drops a Boss Bokoblin Horn (a solid attack-Fuse material) and a Boss Bokoblin Fang; the tougher blue, black, and silver versions also drop the rarer Boss Bokoblin Guts.

enemies.name: Hinox
enemies.tactic: One-eyed giant; sleeps with weapons on its necklace — climb on and grab them first. Shoot the eye to topple it, rush in and melee, then back off before it rises.
enemies.drops: Hinox Horn, Hinox Guts, Hinox Toenail, Hinox Tooth
enemies.battle: Bring a strong Fused one-handed weapon (a heavy monster horn or sturdy rock on a one-hander hits hard and lets you swing fast between knockdowns), a spare bow or two with plenty of arrows for the eye, and a meal that boosts attack or defense; no element is needed for a plain Hinox. They usually lie sleeping out in the open with weapons hung on their necklace, so sneak up first, climb onto the belly, and lift those weapons off for free loot before it wakes. The fight is all about its one giant eye: hit the eye with an arrow and the Hinox topples backward, stunned for a few seconds, so sprint in and mash attacks on its face or feet while it can't fight back, then back off before it rises. Keep a sprint's distance, shoot the eye, rush in, retreat — repeat that loop. Its slow turn buys you time, but don't dawdle right underneath or it'll butt-slam you. At low health it covers the eye with its hand, so shoot faster than it can guard, or wait for the hand to drop. Two extras: a falling/leaping shot (drop from height, not a standing jump) slows time to aim the eye; and some Hinox wear leg armor — burn off wood with a fire arrow, blast metal with a shock arrow. Easy mode for a struggling player: just keep your distance and chain eye-shots — every stun is a free combo and it never gets to swing. It drops great Fuse fodder: Hinox Horn, Hinox Guts, Hinox Toenail, and Hinox Tooth, plus the weapons off its necklace.

enemies.name: Stalnox
enemies.tactic: Skeletal Hinox in caves/the Depths. Shoot its single eye to stagger it for melee; at low health (or with a Bomb Arrow) the eye pops out — smash the loose eyeball or Ultrahand-fling it far from the bones before the body reassembles.
enemies.drops: Stalnox Horn, Hinox Tooth
enemies.battle: Bring a long-reach Fused weapon (a heavy rock or horn on a spear or claymore — the Stalnox is slow, so reach beats speed), a bow with a big stack of arrows, and ideally a few Bomb Arrows. A defense-up or attack-up meal helps; if this Stalnox is in the Depths, pack a Gloom-resistant dish and some Sundelion food to heal gloom hearts, and toss Brightbloom Seeds to light the area first. It rises from a pile of bones into a one-eyed skeleton (on the surface only at night, 9PM–5AM; underground it's already active), so open by shooting an arrow straight into its single eye — that staggers it and drops it to the ground. While it's down, run in and pound the body with melee. When it stands, shoot the eye again and repeat; once its health is low an eye-shot pops the eye clean out (a Bomb Arrow knocks it loose instantly, no matter its health) and the body collapses into bones. The detached eyeball bounces around with its own health bar — chase it down and smash it before the skeleton reassembles. Cheese it: there's no time-stop in this game, but jump off a ledge and aim mid-air to trigger slow-motion for the eye shots, and once the eye drops you can grab it with Ultrahand and fling it far from the bones — dragged off and kept away, the Stalnox can't rebuild and is finished. Its drops are excellent fuse material — the Stalnox Horn is a heavy-hitter (about +29 attack fused), plus a Hinox Tooth.

enemies.name: Stone Talus (incl. Luminous / Rare)
enemies.tactic: Rock golem with an ore weak spot on its back. Run underneath and Ascend straight up onto its top, then hammer the black ore with a blunt/mining weapon. Luminous/Rare variants drop gems.
enemies.drops: Flint, Amber, Luminous Stone; gems like Topaz/Diamond/Ruby/Sapphire on rare types
enemies.battle: Bring your heaviest blunt weapon — a rock or boulder Fused onto a sturdy handle (the same kind of hammer you'd use to mine ore) chews through its stone armor far better than a sword, and a meal that boosts attack helps you finish before it shakes you off. A bow with a few arrows is handy for chipping the weak spot from the ground if you can't climb. The fight starts when this "rock" suddenly stands up; the glowing dark ore deposit on its back is the only weak spot — nothing else does damage. The clean opening is to run right underneath it and use Ascend to phase straight up through its body onto its top, then hammer the ore. Expect about two combos before it tries to buck you off — when it does, just drop down, Ascend up again, and repeat until the ore breaks. It'll also chuck its rock arm at you; the easy, kid-friendly fallback is to use Recall on the thrown rock so it flies right back — that deals no damage but knocks the Talus flat for a few seconds, so you can climb up and wail on it while it's down. Standard ones drop Flint and Amber; the Luminous variant also gives Luminous Stone, and a Rare Talus rains gems — Topaz, Opal, Ruby, Sapphire, even Diamond — making it well worth hunting.

enemies.name: Frox
enemies.tactic: Giant Depths frog-toad. Stun its single eye (arrow, Dazzle Fruit, or a Bomb Flower in its mouth) to topple it, climb on from behind and smash the glowing Zonaite ore on its back with a two-handed weapon. Re-stun and repeat.
enemies.drops: Frox Fang, Frox Fingernail, Frox Guts, Zonaite, Large Zonaite, Crystallized Charge
enemies.battle: Froxes live in the pitch-dark Depths, so first toss Brightbloom Seeds to light the area, eat a hearty meal for extra hearts, and ideally a defense- or attack-up dish (bring Depths armor and Sundelion food if Gloom is around). The real essentials: a heavy two-handed weapon (a Fused rock or boss horn on a claymore smashes the ore fastest), a bow with arrows, and a few Dazzle Fruit or Bomb Flowers. The fight opens with the Frox lunging and trying to suck you in with its huge mouth, so keep your distance. To topple it, hit its single big eye: shoot it with an arrow, fuse or throw a Dazzle Fruit (the bright flash blinds and stuns it harder), or shoot a Bomb Flower into its open mouth so it blows up inside. Once it slumps, climb onto its back (easiest from behind, near its tail) and hammer the glowing Zonaite ore deposits with your two-handed weapon. You usually can't break them all in one go; when it wakes it throws you off, so paraglide, draw your bow for slow-motion, and arrow the eye again to drop it and remount. Repeat until every ore chunk is gone. Easy fallback: stay back and re-stun the eye before each climb so it never gets a turn. Drops are great fuse and Zonai fodder: Frox Fang, Frox Fingernail, Frox Guts, plus Zonaite, Large Zonaite, and Crystallized Charge.

enemies.name: Flux Construct
enemies.tactic: Blocky cube golem — find the green block with the glowing red eye and Ultrahand-pull it (or shoot it) to collapse the body, then pound the weak spot. In platform form, Recall a fallen cube or Ascend up to it. I/II/III scale up and hide the block more.
enemies.drops: Flux Construct Core, Zonaite, Large Zonaite
enemies.battle: Bring a sturdy fused melee weapon (a rock or boulder fused to a claymore hits hard), a bow with a good stack of arrows, and a meal that boosts attack or defense to soak the big slams; no element is required for this fight. It opens as a tall, blocky golem — find the one cube that's green with a glowing red eye, that's its only weak spot. Core loop: shoot the eye with an arrow to stagger it (this stuns it for several seconds), then rush in and pound that block with melee; or, even better, aim Ultrahand at the glowing block and yank it straight off, which collapses the whole body so you can wail on the exposed weak spot while it's down. When it shifts into a floating-platform form and scatters cubes, use Recall on a fallen cube to ride it back up to the body, or use Ascend to pop up underneath it; in its rolling cube form, wait for the eye to rotate toward you, then pull or shoot it. The tougher II and III hide and reposition the glowing block — a leaping midair bow shot triggers slow-motion to line it up. Easy fallback: stay back and let Ultrahand do the work — keep ripping blocks off until it crumbles, then grab and rip the glowing core away and finish it on the ground; the slams only hurt if you're standing right under it. Its prize is the Flux Construct Core — a powerful fuse material you must attach to a weapon or shield on the spot (it can't be carried in your inventory) — plus a pile of Zonai Charges (Large Zonai Charges from a III) and Zonai devices.

enemies.name: Molduga
enemies.tactic: Sand shark in the Gerudo dunes. Stand on a rock off the sand, throw an item or fire an arrow to lure it up, then hit it mid-air with a Bomb-Flower-fused arrow to stun and drop it — sprint in and pound its head while it's beached.
enemies.drops: Molduga Fin, Molduga Jaw, Molduga Guts
enemies.battle: A Molduga lives in the Gerudo Desert, so bring heat resistance first — Desert Voe armor or a chilly/heat-resist dish (it turns cold at night, so a warming dish helps then too) — plus a bow with at least a dozen arrows, a stack of Bomb Flowers (buy them from Fyson in Tarrey Town) to Fuse on, and a sturdy melee weapon; a hearty or defense-up meal cushions its tail swipe. The fight opens with the shark hidden under the sand — you can't hit it down there, and standing on open sand is dangerous, so first climb onto a rock or pillar in the arena and stay off the sand. It hunts by sound and bait, so from safety toss an item or fire an arrow into the dunes; a "?"/"!" appears over its mound and it leaps up to eat, exposing its body. The core loop: when it jumps, hit it with a Bomb-Flower-fused arrow — the blast stuns it and drops it onto the sand — then sprint in and pound its head, where its weak eyes are, until it starts to recover; back off before the spinning tail swipe and burrow. Retreat to high ground and repeat the lure-bomb-beat cycle. If it's overwhelming you or your kid, just camp on the rock and play slow — lure, bomb-arrow, a few head hits, retreat, heal, repeat; patience beats it even with weaker gear. Its drops — Molduga Fin, Molduga Jaw, and Molduga Guts — are excellent, high-value Fuse materials worth the trouble.

enemies.name: King Gleeok
enemies.tactic: Toughest Gleeok — three heads, three elements. Bring a multi-shot Lynel bow, lots of arrows, and Keese-eyeball homing arrows; shoot every head's eye in mid-air bullet-time to crash it down and melee the heads, then chase it in the aerial storm with updrafts/Recall.
enemies.drops: King Gleeok Horn, Wing, Guts
enemies.battle: Bring a multi-shot bow (a Lynel or Savage Lynel bow that fires 3 or 5 arrows at once) and a big stack of arrows, plus Keese Eyeballs to Fuse to them — eyeball arrows home in and can tag the three heads quickly. Carry a hard-hitting melee weapon for when it lands (something with a strong monster part or a Lynel horn Fused on), an attack- or defense-boosting meal, and a fairy or two for emergencies; if you're fighting the Depths version, eat Sunny food or wear gloom-resistant gear. Each head channels its own element (fire, frost, thunder) and its eye is the weak spot — matching the opposite element helps, but homing eyeball arrows work on all three. The opening: as it rears up, jump or use a Zonai spring to get height, pull your bow in mid-air so time slows, and shoot each head's eye — every head has its own bar, and they regenerate if you're slow. Tulin's gust pushes you farther across the air for a better angle. Knock all three heads down and King Gleeok crashes to the ground stunned — sprint in and wail on the heads with your melee weapon, then repeat. When it's low it flies high and unleashes all three elements at once in a storm; ride the updraft from its thunderstrikes with your paraglider, use Recall on a thrown ice spike to ride it back up, or pop a Rocket, to get one more slow-motion volley off. The safe, kid-friendly route is patience: stay near cover, only pop out to fire homing eyeball arrows, and never melee until all three heads are down. Its drops — the Gleeok Flame, Frost, and Thunder Horns plus the Gleeok Wing and Guts — are top-tier fuse materials worth the fight.

enemies.name: Lynel
enemies.tactic: Brutal centaur. Shoot the face to stun, mount it for free hits, and flurry-rush its charge. Fuse a Lynel horn for a devastating weapon. Bring backup weapons and food first.
enemies.drops: Lynel Saber Horn or Mace Horn, Lynel Hoof, Lynel Guts, Lynel weapons
enemies.battle: Lynels are Tears of the Kingdom's true skill check, but very beatable once you commit. Bring several strong weapons (a Lynel Saber Horn, a heavy rock, or a sturdy Construct part Fused to a one-handed sword hits hard and lets you land the most blows in a flurry), a bow with plenty of arrows, a 30+ guard shield, and a meal that boosts attack or defense plus a stack of healing food — a weapon or two will break before it dies. Open with a face shot from stealth or range: an arrow to its head makes the Lynel reel for a couple seconds, so sprint behind it and hit the on-screen mount prompt to climb on its back and mash attack for free hits (no durability lost, though it drains your stamina) until it bucks you off. On the ground, bait its charge and side-hop at the last instant for a flurry rush, or raise your shield as it swings for a perfect guard, which briefly stuns it; stay right on top of it so it can't use its fireball or three-to-five-shot arrow volleys, and re-stun and remount when you can. The easy fallback: keep the sage Tulin active for bonus headshots, throw Puffshrooms to blind it in smoke while you reposition, and play patient defense, dodging for flurries instead of trading hits. A defeated Lynel always drops both its Saber Horn and Mace Horn, plus hooves, guts, and its weapon, bow, and shield — some of the best Fuse materials in the game.

enemies.name: Demon King Ganondorf
enemies.tactic: Three-phase finale: flurry-rush his combos with all five sages, survive the one-on-one where he dodges back, then skydive onto the Demon Dragon. Pack Sunny dishes (Sundelions) to heal gloom.
enemies.battle: Bring your strongest Fused melee weapons (a Silver Lynel horn or a heavy blade on a claymore hits hard), a sturdy shield, and the Master Sword if you have it (it deals bonus damage to gloom). Most important: cook several Sunny dishes with Sundelions (found on Sky Islands), because his attacks inflict gloom that locks away your hearts and only Sunny meals heal it back — also pack an attack-up or defense-up dish and plenty of hearts. Have all five sages recruited first. The fight opens against waves of gloom-infused monsters while your sages fight beside you. Then comes the duel: the core loop is flurry rush — wait for his lunging thrust, dodge it at the last instant (or backflip his wide sweep), press Y to flurry, then trigger your sages' vows to pile on extra damage; repeat. Next he releases phantom clones — let the sages tie up the copies while you focus the real one. After that he absorbs the clones and knocks your sages out, going one-on-one: now he perfect-dodges your strikes and even your flurries, so bait a dodge and punish his counter to land hits, and watch for the gloom projectiles he fires before attacks (a good bow helps, since he can't dodge arrows). Beat him and he swallows his Secret Stone to become the Demon Dragon — a final aerial phase: skydive from Zelda's Light Dragon onto its back and smash the glowing weak points until Zelda ends it. The safe play for a struggling player: keep your distance, only flurry his obvious thrust, eat a Sunny dish the moment gloom eats your hearts, and never get greedy. He drops no materials — the reward is the story's end.

enemies.name: Demon Dragon
enemies.tactic: Final form, fought in free-fall. Dive onto its back and hit the four glowing weak spots with the Master Sword (only it damages them, 10x dmg); the Light Dragon catches you between each, then finish the Secret Stone on its head.
enemies.battle: This last fight is more spectacle than danger, so don't panic — you can't fall to your death here, because Zelda's Light Dragon always swoops in to catch you. The one must-have is the Master Sword: the Demon Dragon's black scales repel every other weapon, and only the Master Sword damages its glowing weak spots, hitting them for huge bonus damage (no element is needed). It helps to have your paraglider and a few hearts of buffer (a hearty or sunny meal) in case its Gloom breath or projectiles graze you. The fight opens with you riding the Light Dragon high above the Demon Dragon; dive off and aim for one of the glowing eye-like weak spots on its back. The core loop is simple: free-fall onto a weak spot and slash it with the Master Sword until it bursts, then glide toward the Light Dragon's head — it homes in, catches you, lifts you back up for the next one, and even heals any Gloom you've taken. Clear all four back weak spots that way. If diving onto them is fiddly, you can also shoot arrows at the spots on the way down — easy for a kid to manage. Once the back is done, the Secret Stone on its head glows; glide down, strike it with the Master Sword, then hit the final-blow prompt to finish the Demon Dragon and the game. There are no drops — the reward is the ending.

enemies.name: Fire Gleeok
enemies.tactic: Three fire heads; arrow each one (ice-fused does the most) to stagger and ground it, then melee — ride its fireball updraft for a slow-mo aerial volley.
enemies.drops: Gleeok Flame Horn, Wing, Guts
enemies.battle: The Flame Gleeok is one of TotK's scariest sky bosses, but it's weak to ice, so the fight melts fast once you come prepared. Its lair runs dangerously hot, so first eat a heat-resistance meal (or wear a Desert Voe piece) — then add a defense-up or hearty dish and pack ~40 arrows. Bring a strong melee weapon for the downed phase and a multi-shot bow, and fuse arrows with an icy material — Ice Fruit or White Chuchu Jelly; adding a Keese Eyeball makes arrows home in on the heads so you barely have to aim. The whole fight is about its three heads: land an arrow on each (knock all three out before they regenerate) to stagger it and bring it crashing down, then sprint in and unload melee on the grounded heads before it lifts off. Repeat. When it's low it floats up and drops a giant fireball — guard or dodge the blast, then ride the updraft its flames leave behind with your paraglider (Vow of Tulin's gust pushes you higher). Draw your bow in mid-air to trigger slow-motion, and pop all three heads at once. It drops the Gleeok Wing, the rare Gleeok Guts, and the Gleeok Flame Horn — a +30 flaming fuse material.

enemies.name: Frost Gleeok
enemies.tactic: Three ice heads, weak to fire: stun all three eyes with fire/homing arrows to drop it, then melee.
enemies.drops: Frost Gleeok Horn, Wing, Guts
enemies.battle: Bring FIRE and cold gear first. Fuse arrows with Fire Fruit (or a Fire-Breath Lizalfos Horn) so each hit does bonus damage to this frost monster, and pack a multishot bow plus a big stack of arrows. Wear cold-resistant armor (an upgraded Snowquill set) so its icy breath doesn't freeze you, and eat an attack-up or defense-up meal before you start. Opening: its three heads breathe ice, so don't charge in. Stay at range and shoot each head in the eye — fusing a Keese Eyeball to an arrow makes the shot home in, so you can tag the eyes from the ground. Core loop: stun all three heads and the whole beast crashes down; sprint in and unload your strongest weapon before it recovers, then back off and repeat. To hit all three heads in one draw, jump off your horse mid-gallop, or leap from a high ledge (Ascend up something first) to trigger slow-motion bullet-time aiming; Tulin's gust can give you extra glide height. When it drops to about a third health it flies up and rains giant icicles — climb onto one stuck in the ground and use Recall to ride it skyward, back into eye range. Easy fallback: a multishot bow plus homing Keese-eyeball arrows lets you spam the eyes from safety. Defeating it drops a Gleeok Frost Horn and Gleeok Wing — top-tier fuse materials (a Gleeok Frost Horn adds +30 attack and an ice burst) — and sometimes Gleeok Guts.

enemies.name: Thunder Gleeok
enemies.tactic: Three shock heads, no elemental weakness; avoid metal gear, disable all three heads at once to stun it.
enemies.drops: Thunder Gleeok Horn, Wing, Guts
enemies.battle: Bring a multishot bow and lots of arrows (this fight is mostly archery), wooden or Zonaite weapons rather than metal ones — its storm calls lightning down onto metal gear — plus shock resistance (a meal/elixir or the Rubber armor set) and an attack- or defense-up dish. Unlike the Flame and Frost Gleeoks it has no elemental weakness, so don't rely on an element — the goal is to knock out its three shock-spitting heads. Fusing Keese Eyeballs onto your arrows makes them home in on those heads, and Tulin's gust helps you get airborne and close distance. Open by climbing or using Ascend to reach high ground, then glide off and draw your bow in mid-air — slow-mo lets you disable all three heads in one volley, which stuns the Gleeok and drops it. While it's down, sprint in and wail on the heads with your strongest melee weapon until it recovers, then back off and headshot it again. Around two-thirds health it flies up and rains thunderbolts, but each strike leaves a tall wind updraft you can ride with the paraglider to keep firing into the heads and bring it back down. If you're struggling, just spam Keese-Eyeball-fused arrows at the heads from a distance and ride the updrafts between volleys — no melee risk. Its drops — the Gleeok Thunder Horn, Gleeok Wing, and Gleeok Guts — are among the best fuse and elixir materials in the game.

enemies.name: Colgera
enemies.tactic: Wind Temple boss fought while skydiving — fire-fuse your arrows and shoot its three ice-covered weak segments.
enemies.drops: Heart Container, Tulin's Vow
enemies.battle: This is a skydiving boss, so the gear that matters most is a sturdy bow (pack a backup bow too) and a big stack of arrows. Wear cold-resistance armor or eat a spicy/warm dish for the freezing Hebra air, and bring hearty or attack-up food to top off hearts. Fire is the key: fuse a Fire Fruit or Red Chuchu Jelly to your arrows to crack Colgera's ice fast. The fight opens with you diving off the temple into open air; aim at the three rounded, ice-covered segments along its head, body, and tail. Each weak point takes two hits — one to break the icy shell, one to pop the exposed core — and with fire-fused arrows that's about two arrows per segment (around three without fire). Destroying all three ends phase one. Don't panic about stamina: opening your paraglider to glide refills it, so dive to aim, then glide to recover and line up the next shot. Get below or behind it and shoot the segments as you fall; when it flings its ice spikes it briefly exposes the weak points, so punish that. In phase two it regrows the segments and adds slow-moving tornadoes — keep your distance to weave between them, and when it vanishes into a bubble it reappears from below for a pincer strike, so use Tulin's gust to reposition out of the way, then resume hitting the three weak spots. The patient, kid-friendly approach works fine: keep gliding to refill stamina, never run dry on arrows, and chip one segment at a time from a safe distance. Beating it grants a Heart Container and the Vow of Tulin, the Sage of Wind, whose gust ability you'll lean on constantly afterward.

enemies.name: Marbled Gohma
enemies.tactic: Fire Temple boss; charge Yunobo at its legs to knock it down, then climb up and smash the eye.
enemies.drops: Heart Container, Yunobo's Vow
enemies.battle: Wear Flamebreaker armor (or sip a Fireproof Elixir) for the Fire Temple heat, bring your strongest Fused melee weapon — a rock or heavy material on a claymore lands big eye hits — and pack an attack-up or defense-up cooked meal. The real key is Yunobo: you fight this alongside the young Goron, so aim and fire his rolling charge at Marbled Gohma. Phase one is the floor phase — its body is wrapped in marbled rock you can't dent, so blast two of its legs with Yunobo's charge and the whole thing collapses, exposing its big eye. Climb up (or use Ascend) and wail on the eye with your strong weapon, or shoot it with arrows, until it recovers, then repeat. When it spits or drops explosive marbled rocks, move out of the blast — or pop them with Yunobo. At about half health it clings to the ceiling: aim Yunobo up the room's curved grey walls so he rolls across the ceiling and slams the boss — a single good hit crumbles all its legs and drops it, then climb and hit the eye again. Its nasty move here is a ring of explosive boulders dropped around you; smash one with Yunobo, or use Recall to lift one and slip through the gap, then run clear before they blow. If the climbing is too fiddly for a kid, lean on Yunobo for the legs and only commit to the eye when it's flat on the ground. Win and you get a Heart Container plus the Vow of Yunobo, Sage of Fire — the ability to call his charge anywhere from then on.

enemies.name: Mucktorok
enemies.tactic: Water Temple boss; strip the sludge shark with water (Sidon's shield or Splash-Fruit arrows), then flip and pound the octopus inside.
enemies.drops: Heart Container, Sidon's Vow
enemies.battle: Bring Splash Fruit and Chuchu Jelly to fuse onto your arrows (water strips the sludge off the boss), a strong fused melee weapon for the punish windows, and plenty of arrows; a spear is handy here for landing quick multi-hits while it's exposed. Eat an attack-up or defense-up dish, and keep Sidon summoned the whole fight, since his water shield washes off sludge and blocks its muddy attacks. The fight opens with the Mucktorok charging around the arena disguised in a shark-shaped sludge suit, ramming you or firing a sludge beam; you can't hurt the suit, so hit it with water to strip it. The core loop: splash it with Sidon's water shield (one hit) or a water-fused arrow (about two hits) to knock off the sludge and expose the little octopus inside, fire an arrow to flip it over, then rush in and unload with melee before it re-coats. Below half health it floods the floor with sludge puddles that slow you and dives in to flee, so shoot it with a water arrow the instant it jumps out. The kid-friendly cheese: this arena has low gravity, so jump to trigger slow-motion, calmly land water-fused arrows on it, and lean on Sidon's shield to clear sludge for free. Winning earns a Heart Container and the Vow of Sidon, Sage of Water, his water ability.

enemies.name: Queen Gibdo
enemies.tactic: Lightning Temple boss; hit her with Riju's lightning (or fire/cold) to turn her white, then strike — destroy the Gibdo hives in phase 2.
enemies.drops: Heart Container, Riju's Vow
enemies.battle: Queen Gibdo is the Lightning Temple boss, and the fight is built around Riju's lightning, so step onto the boss-room warp pad with her in your party. Bring a bow and plenty of arrows, a strong melee weapon (a one-handed weapon or spear fused with a hard-hitting material for the close-up windows), and a defense-up or hearty meal, since her attacks hit hard. She is weak to shock, fire, and cold; a lightning or fire hit turns her white and vulnerable. To activate Riju's power, stay near her, draw your bow to call up the lightning target, and fire an arrow into it to call down a strike. Land that hit to turn her white, then sprint in and unload your strongest melee combo; a follow-up lightning hit while she's white knocks her down for free damage, so back off and repeat. Dodge her sand tornadoes by dashing to the side, and when she fires the tracking sand beam just hold ZL to block it. Around half health she starts a diving attack and summons Gibdo from hives around the arena — destroy the hives (Riju's lightning or explosive-fused arrows) and stand in the pillars of light, which make wandering Gibdo immobile and destroy them. If you're struggling, lean on Riju: re-aim a fresh lightning strike for every stun and chip her down safely from range. Beating her grants a Heart Container and Riju's Vow, her lightning sage ability.

basics.title: Fuse makes your weapons
basics.body: The weapons you find are weak and decayed, so on their own they barely scratch anything. Press the Fuse ability (the green hand) to stick a monster horn, a rock, or a gem onto a weapon to massively boost its damage, or onto a shield or arrow to add an effect. A Lynel or Gleeok horn or a heavy rock turns a flimsy stick into a real weapon, and this is the single most important habit in combat.

basics.title: Flurry Rush
basics.body: Hold up your guard (ZL) and dodge at the very last second to trigger slow-motion, then mash Y for a free combo. Hop sideways to dodge an overhead or charging swing or a thrust, and backflip (pull back) to dodge a horizontal sideways swing.

basics.title: Perfect Guard (Parry)
basics.body: Hold up your shield with ZL, then press A right as a blow lands to deflect it instead of just blocking. It's great against chargers and against projectiles you can knock back at the enemy.

basics.title: Sneakstrike & weak points
basics.body: Crouch and sneak up on an enemy that hasn't noticed you (watch the noise meter) for a big bonus hit before the fight even starts. A bow shot to the head or eye staggers most enemies, and aiming your bow while falling through the air (off a ledge or your paraglider, not a standing jump) slows time so you can line up that shot.

basics.title: Elements: fire, ice, shock, water
basics.body: Fire burns enemies and creates an updraft you can paraglide up, frost freezes a foe so the next hit shatters them, and shock makes enemies drop their gear and is deadly to anyone holding metal. Water and Splash Fruit wash off sludge and put out fire, so Fuse the element that exploits a foe's weakness. Never carry a metal weapon or shield in a thunderstorm or near a Thunder Gleeok, or lightning will hit you.

basics.title: Weapons break — that's normal
basics.body: Every weapon wears out and breaks, so don't hoard your best one; carry several and use them. When a fused weapon is almost gone, throw it for a burst of damage, then Fuse a fresh one from the endless monster parts you keep picking up.

basics.title: What to bring to a hard fight
basics.body: Cook one defense-up or attack-up meal, bring a couple of strong Fused weapons and a sturdy shield to parry, and carry a bow with a stack of arrows plus a few you can Fuse an element onto. If you're heading into the Depths, pack Brightbloom Seeds for light. That's it.

BESTIARY.notes: Adversarial verify done against Game8, ScreenRant/RankedBoost, GameRant, PowerPyx, Zelda Dungeon, Kiranico, Fextralife. Corrections made to the original dataset: (1) Stalnox drop "Ancient Bone" was wrong/invented; real drops are Stalnox Horn + Hinox Tooth. (2) Like Like listed "Sticky Frog" as a drop, which is invented (Sticky Frog is a critter caught near water, not a monster drop); corrected to Opal + chest gear, with Ice Like Stone/Rock Like Stone from variants. (3) Frox was missing its "Frox Fang" drop and listed vague "gems"; corrected to Frox Fang/Fingernail/Guts + Zonaite/Large Zonaite/Crystallized Charge. (4) Boss Bokoblin drop "Bokoblin Horn (high tier)" corrected to the real materials Boss Bokoblin Horn + Boss Bokoblin Guts. (5) Construct drop "Zonaite" corrected to "Zonai Charge" (the standard Construct drop). (6) Lynel "Lynel Horn" corrected to the real horn names Lynel Saber Horn / Mace Horn. (7) Phantom Ganon drop expanded to include the Gloom weapon (Sword/Club/Spear) it was wielding, alongside the always-dropped Demon King's Bow and Dark Clump. (8) Aerocuda tactic edited (Keese Eyeball-fused arrow homes in). (9) Gibdo tactic refined: they harden/revive and are broken by any element (fire/electric/ice), not specifically a fused Gibdo Bone. (10) Hinox drops expanded (Tooth also drops). (11) Stone Talus renamed to Luminous/Rare variants with accurate gem list. (12) The grouped "Seized/Marbled bosses" entry renamed to the actual four temple bosses. Verified-accurate as written (no change): Bokoblin, Moblin, Lizalfos, Horriblin, Molduga, all Gleeok elemental counters, Flux Construct weak-block, Frox eye/ore cycle, Demon Dragon (4 back weak spots, Master Sword 10x dmg, Light Dragon lifts you between hits), Gloom Hands light/Bomb-arrow tactic. Tiers kept as: common, mini-boss (field bosses), boss (region/major), construct (Zonai automata), gloom (gloom-type). Lower confidence: exact full drop tables are representative not exhaustive (higher-tier color variants add Guts and extra gems/Zonaite). All step/tactic/drop strings kept under 170 chars, plain text.

rules: One effect per dish: mixing two different effect ingredients (e.g. Spicy + Hasty) cancels both, leaving a plain meal that only heals.
Stack the same prefix to go further: more same-effect ingredients raise the tier (Spicy to Spicy to Hot-Footed style) and lengthen the timer.
Cook in a lit Cooking Pot. Holding ingredients and dropping them in a campfire or open flame does NOT make effect meals.
Elixirs need a critter plus a monster part: e.g. Hightail Lizard + monster part = Hasty Elixir. No critter or no monster part means no elixir.
Monster parts set elixir duration, not the effect. Tougher monster part = longer timer; the critter decides which effect you get.
Add a Dragon Horn shard (Dinraal/Naydra/Farosh/Light) to any elixir to max the timer to 30 minutes instantly.
Sundelions add the Sunny effect, the only way to cook back gloom-damaged (locked) hearts; Sun Pumpkin also gives Sunny.
Brightcaps and Glowing Cave Fish make Bright meals that give Link a glowing aura: hugely useful in the dark Depths and caves.
Muddle Buds and Dazzlefruit are throw/Fuse materials, not cooking ingredients: they confuse or blind enemies, they do not make meals.
Star fragments and most gems/ores can't be cooked; adding a non-food item usually produces Dubious Food with no effect.
Up to 5 ingredients per dish. Adding more of the same effect food gives more hearts/duration up to the cap.
Hearty-effect dishes fully heal and add temporary yellow (bonus) hearts; they ignore the normal heart count.

effects.does: Fully restores hearts and adds temporary bonus (yellow) hearts on top of your max.
effects.ingredients: Big Hearty Radish, Hearty Radish, Hearty Truffle, Big Hearty Truffle, Hearty Salmon, Hearty Bass.

effects.does: Instantly refills part of your stamina wheel for climbing, gliding, and sprinting.
effects.ingredients: Stamella Shroom, Stambulb, plus Energetic Rhino Beetle for elixirs.
effects.elixir: Energetic Rhino Beetle + any monster part = Energizing Elixir.

effects.does: Overfills stamina past full with extra green wheels (best for long climbs/glides).
effects.ingredients: Endura Carrot, Endura Shroom, plus Tireless Frog for elixirs.
effects.elixir: Tireless Frog + any monster part = Enduring Elixir.

effects.does: Keeps Link warm in cold regions so you don't take freezing chip damage.
effects.ingredients: Spicy Pepper, Sizzlefin Trout, plus Warm Darner for elixirs.
effects.elixir: Warm Darner + any monster part = Spicy Elixir.

effects.does: Stops heat damage in hot areas like the Gerudo Desert and Eldin's hot lowlands. Note: Death Mountain's extreme heat needs Fireproof, not this.
effects.ingredients: Hydromelon, Cool Safflina, Chillfin Trout, plus Cold Darner for elixirs.
effects.elixir: Cold Darner + any monster part = Chilly Elixir.

effects.does: Prevents Link bursting into flame in extreme heat (e.g. Death Mountain). Elixir only.
effects.ingredients: Fireproof Lizard or Smotherwing Butterfly, each plus any monster part.
effects.elixir: Fireproof Lizard (or Smotherwing Butterfly) + any monster part = Fireproof Elixir.

effects.does: Reduces or blocks electric/shock damage and stops weapons being knocked from your hand.
effects.ingredients: Voltfruit, Zapshroom, Electric Safflina, Voltfin Trout, plus Electric Darner for elixirs.
effects.elixir: Electric Darner + any monster part = Electro Elixir.

effects.does: Temporarily raises your attack power so weapons hit harder.
effects.ingredients: Mighty Bananas, Razorshroom, Razorclaw Crab, Mighty Carp, plus Bladed Rhino Beetle for elixirs.
effects.elixir: Bladed Rhino Beetle + any monster part = Mighty Elixir.

effects.does: Temporarily raises defense so you take less damage from hits.
effects.ingredients: Ironshroom, Fortified Pumpkin, Armored Carp, Ironshell Crab, plus Rugged Rhino Beetle for elixirs.
effects.elixir: Rugged Rhino Beetle + any monster part = Tough Elixir.

effects.does: Increases Link's movement and running speed on foot.
effects.ingredients: Swift Carrot, Fleet-Lotus Seeds, Swift Violet, Hyrule Bass, plus Hightail Lizard for elixirs.
effects.elixir: Hightail Lizard + any monster part = Hasty Elixir.

effects.does: Lowers the noise Link makes so enemies and animals are harder to alert.
effects.ingredients: Silent Princess, Blue Nightshade, Silent Shroom, Sneaky River Snail, plus Sunset Firefly for elixirs.
effects.elixir: Sunset Firefly + any monster part = Sneaky Elixir.

effects.does: Prevents slipping while climbing on wet surfaces or in the rain.
effects.ingredients: Sticky Lizard or Sticky Frog, each plus any monster part (elixir only).
effects.elixir: Sticky Lizard (or Sticky Frog) + any monster part = Sticky Elixir.

effects.does: Surrounds Link with a glowing aura that lights the area: vital in the dark Depths and caves.
effects.ingredients: Brightcap, Glowing Cave Fish; a simple Bright meal is Brightcap + Apple.
effects.elixir: Deep Firefly + any monster part = Bright Elixir.

effects.does: Heals normal hearts AND restores gloom-damaged (locked) hearts, which nothing else cooks back.
effects.ingredients: Sundelion (best), Sun Pumpkin; both give the Sunny effect.

recipes.name: Sunny Fried Wild Greens
recipes.makes: Restores gloom-damaged hearts (up to ~15 from Gloom)
recipes.why: Sun Pumpkin + Sundelion. The go-to before Depths runs and gloom-heavy fights, since Sunny is the only gloom cure.

recipes.name: Hearty Steamed Fish
recipes.makes: Full heal plus many temporary bonus hearts
recipes.why: Hearty Salmon or Hearty Bass alone in the pot. Tons of yellow hearts for tough bosses; stack more Hearty fish for more.

recipes.name: Enduring Carrot Stew
recipes.why: Endura Carrots only. Extra green stamina wheels make long climbs and sky-glides trivial; better than plain Energizing.

recipes.name: Mighty Crab Stir-Fry
recipes.why: Razorclaw Crab + Mighty ingredient + Goron Spice/Hylian herbs. Easy, high-tier attack boost for fights and the Depths.

recipes.name: Bright Meal (Brightcap + Apple)
recipes.makes: Glowing aura to light dark areas
recipes.why: Cheap, farmable Brightcaps. Lights your path in the Depths so you can navigate between Lightroots without flares.

recipes.name: Fireproof Elixir
recipes.makes: Flame Guard (no burning in extreme heat)
recipes.why: Fireproof Lizard or Smotherwing Butterfly + any monster part. Needed around Death Mountain before Goron/Fire content.

dragons.name: Dinraal
dragons.where: Circles Eldin and Akkala; enters Depths at Drenan Highlands Chasm, exits at East Akkala Plains Chasm. Catch from Ulri Mountain or Thyphlo Ruins Skyview Tower.
dragons.parts: Scale, Claw, Fang, Horn (shard), Spike. Don't use a wooden bow near it (fire). Fuse for fire weapons; Horn maxes elixir timer to 30 min.

dragons.name: Naydra
dragons.where: Emerges near Mount Lanayru, circles Necluda/Hateno, dives near East Hill above Kakariko. Easy farm from Mount Lanayru Skyview Tower.
dragons.parts: Scale, Claw, Fang, Horn (shard), Spike. She is freezing-cold, so bring cold resistance. Fuse for ice weapons; Horn maxes elixir timer.

dragons.name: Farosh
dragons.where: Circles Gerudo and Faron; enters Depths at Hills of Baumer Chasm, exits at East Gerudo Chasm. Wait at Popla Foothills Skyview Tower or Mount Hylia.
dragons.parts: Scale, Claw, Fang, Horn (shard), Spike. Don't use a metal bow near it (shock). Fuse for electric weapons; Horn maxes elixir timer.

dragons.name: Light Dragon
dragons.where: Circles the entire map at the far edges and a very high altitude; needs Sky islands/towers to reach. (Tied to the main story; spoiler-light here.)
dragons.parts: Scale, Claw, Fang, Horn (shard), Spike. Parts make powerful light/holy fuses and its Horn maxes elixir timers like the others.

COOKING.notes: CORRECTIONS MADE (web-verified vs Game8, Zelda Dungeon, Gamer Rant, June 2026): (1) Removed 'Hearty Durian' from Hearty ingredients — it does NOT exist in TotK (it was a BotW item, cut from this game). Replaced with Big Hearty Truffle, a real TotK Hearty item. (2) Fixed Chilly 'does' line: removed 'Death Mountain foothills' as a Chilly use, since Death Mountain's extreme heat requires the Fireproof effect, not heat resistance — leaving this could send a first-timer to Death Mountain with the wrong dish and get them burned. (3) Corrected Dinraal's Depths entry to the proper in-game name 'Drenan Highlands Chasm' (was 'Drenan Highland Chasm') and clarified exit as 'East Akkala Plains Chasm'. Everything else verified ACCURATE: all effect/critter/elixir mappings (Energetic Rhino Beetle=Energizing, Tireless Frog=Enduring, Warm/Cold/Electric Darner, Hightail Lizard, Bladed/Rugged Rhino Beetle, Sunset Firefly, Deep Firefly=Bright Elixir, Fireproof Lizard/Smotherwing Butterfly, Sticky Lizard/Frog); Spicy=cold resist and Chilly=heat resist (correctly assigned, not swapped); the cooking rules (two effects cancel to a plain heal, same-prefix stacking, lit pot required, critter+monster part for elixirs, monster part sets duration, Dragon Horn shard = 30-min max for all four dragons including Light); Sundelion/Sun Pumpkin=Sunny gloom cure (Sundelion ~3 hearts each, Sun Pumpkin ~1); Brightcap/Glowing Cave Fish=Bright; Muddle Bud/Dazzlefruit being throw/Fuse not cooking; all four dragon routes and farming towers (Mount Lanayru, Ulri Mountain, Thyphlo Ruins, Popla Foothills towers all real). Lower confidence remains on the exact 'best' single critter per effect since many share an effect — listed ones are confirmed correct, just not guaranteed the absolute optimum. All steps kept under 170 chars, plain text, real in-game proper names. Light Dragon kept deliberately spoiler-aware.

systems.name: The three layers: Surface, Sky, Depths
systems.what: Hyrule has three stacked worlds. Sky has floating islands and Skyview Towers. The Depths sit beneath the Surface, dark and full of Gloom.

systems.name: The Depths and Gloom
systems.what: The Depths are pitch-black. Toss Brightbloom Seeds or hit Lightroots to light them. Gloom damages and locks hearts until you reach light or eat a sundelion dish.

systems.name: Lightroots mirror shrines
systems.what: Activating a Lightroot lights a chunk of the Depths and marks the map. All 120 Lightroots sit under the 120 Surface shrines, names spelled backwards.

systems.name: Poes and Bargainer Statues
systems.what: Poes are blue flames found in the Depths; they are currency. Spend them at the seven Bargainer Statues to buy Gloom-resistant gear and items.

systems.name: Shrines and Light of Blessing
systems.what: There are 152 shrines across Surface and Sky. Each gives a Light of Blessing. Trade 4 at any Goddess Statue for one heart or one stamina vessel.

systems.name: Heart and stamina swap
systems.what: At the Horned Statue in the bunker beneath Lookout Landing you can rebalance hearts and stamina. It costs rupees: sell an essence for 100, buy the other back for 120.

systems.name: Battery and Energy Cells
systems.what: Energy Cells power Zonai devices. Mine Zonaite in the Depths, trade 3 to a Forge Construct for a Crystallized Charge, then add cells at a Crystal Refinery.

systems.name: Skyview Towers
systems.what: 15 Skyview Towers reveal the regional map and launch you skyward. Open the shutters by solving each tower's small puzzle, then get fired into the Sky.

systems.name: Zonai devices and Autobuild
systems.what: Zonai devices (fans, wheels, rockets) attach with Ultrahand. Autobuild rebuilds saved or recent creations from materials, spending Zonaite for missing parts.

systems.name: Great Fairies
systems.what: Four Great Fairies (Tera, Cotera, Kaysa, Mija) upgrade armor. Unlock each by reuniting the Stable Trotters troupe, led by conductor Mastro, near a stable.

systems.name: Korok seeds and Hestu
systems.what: Find Korok seeds (900 total) and give them to Hestu to expand your weapon, bow, and shield pouches. Many hide behind carry-a-Korok-to-its-friend puzzles.

systems.name: Dragon's Tears and geoglyphs
systems.what: Visit the geoglyphs drawn across the Surface to unlock the Dragon's Tears memories, telling the Imprisoning War story out of order.

systems.name: Sage abilities and Sage's Will
systems.what: The five sages grant abilities you trigger by their avatar (Tulin gusts, Yunobo charges, etc.). Offer 4 Sage's Wills at a Goddess Statue to boost one sage (about 1.3x).

systems.name: The four temples and the fifth sage
systems.what: Wind (Rito/Tulin), Fire (Goron/Yunobo), Water (Zora/Sidon), Lightning (Gerudo/Riju). Mineru, the fifth sage, comes from the Spirit Temple.

upgrades: Hearts and stamina: collect Lights of Blessing from shrines (152 total) and trade 4 at a Goddess Statue for one heart container or one stamina vessel.
Heart/stamina swap: at the Horned Statue in the Lookout Landing bunker you can rebalance later. It is not free; each swap nets about 20 rupees (sell 100, rebuy 120).
Battery (Energy Cells): mine Zonaite in the Depths, trade 3 Zonaite per Crystallized Charge at a Forge Construct, then spend 100 charges per cell at a Crystal Refinery.
Pouch slots: hand Korok seeds to Hestu to add weapon, bow, and shield inventory slots; costs climb as you expand.
Armor: upgrade gear at any of the four Great Fairy fountains once unlocked, spending materials and rupees per level.
Sage power: collect Sage's Wills (20 exist; some guarded by Gleeoks) and offer 4 at a Goddess Statue to strengthen a chosen sage's ability about 1.3x.

fairies.name: Tera
fairies.location: Near Woodland Stable in Eldin, by Pico Pond. You meet conductor Mastro and the Stable Trotters here; awaken Tera first to unlock the others.
fairies.cost: Free to unlock (reunite the Stable Trotters); armor upgrades then cost materials plus rupees

fairies.name: Cotera
fairies.location: Near Dueling Peaks Stable, south of Kakariko Village on the east side of Dueling Peaks.
fairies.cost: Free to unlock (reunite the next Stable Trotters musician); upgrades cost materials plus rupees

fairies.name: Kaysa
fairies.location: Near Outskirt Stable, southwest edge of Hyrule Field, west of the Coliseum Ruins.
fairies.cost: Free to unlock (reunite the Stable Trotters); upgrades cost materials plus rupees

fairies.name: Mija
fairies.location: Near Snowfield Stable in the South Tabantha Snowfield (cold region; pack warm gear).
fairies.cost: Free to unlock (final Stable Trotters performance); upgrades cost materials plus rupees

WORLD.notes: Web-verified against Nintendo Life, Game8, GameWith, ZeldaDungeon, Fextralife. CORRECTIONS to the source dataset: (1) The heart/stamina swap is done at the HORNED STATUE (also nicknamed the Cursed Statue), hidden in the Emergency Shelter bunker beneath Lookout Landing, unlocked via the "Who Goes There?!" quest. It is NOT a Goddess Statue and NOT a Bargainer/"Statue of the Dead" (those are the separate Poe-trading statues). (2) The swap is NOT free: you sell an essence for 100 rupees and rebuy the other type for 120, a net 20 rupees per swap. The source's own note that recommended dropping the Horned Statue reference was backwards and has been reversed. (3) Korok seed total corrected from 1000 to 900 (giving all to Hestu fully maxes pouches; the famous "1000th" is a meme/extra, but the real count is 900). (4) Softened "press A" sage-activation wording since activation differs per sage (Tulin gust on glide, Yunobo charge on aim+attack, etc.); walking up + prompt is the secondary cue. Confirmed accurate as given: 152 shrines, 120 Lightroots (names spelled backwards under Surface shrines), 15 Skyview Towers, 7 Bargainer Statues (1 Surface + 6 Depths), 20 Sage's Wills (some guarded by King Gleeoks), 4 Sage's Wills per upgrade at ~1.3x, 4 Lights of Blessing per vessel, 3 Zonaite per Crystallized Charge at a Forge Construct, 100 charges per Energy Cell at the Crystal Refinery, 4 Great Fairies (Tera, Cotera, Kaysa, Mija) unlocked via the Stable Trotters troupe led by conductor Mastro, Tera awakened first. Medium-high confidence on stable pairings; Tera-first ordering is firm. Note: Korok total of 900 is well documented but some count the bonus, so if your app already standardized on 900 keep it.

RUNES.name: Ultrahand
RUNES.from: Ukouh Shrine, your first shrine on the Great Sky Island.
RUNES.what: Grab, move, rotate and glue almost any object together to build bridges, vehicles, and machines from Zonai parts.
RUNES.tip: Hold the button to fine-tune angle and distance; objects snap and turn yellow when they'll stick. Press up on the D-pad to detach mistakes.

RUNES.name: Fuse
RUNES.from: In-isa Shrine, the second Great Sky Island shrine.
RUNES.what: Attach an object or material to a weapon, shield, or arrow to boost damage, change its effect, or add reach.
RUNES.tip: Fuse a rock onto a flimsy stick for a hammer, or a Keese eyeball onto arrows for homing shots. It saves your weak weapons from breaking fast.

RUNES.name: Ascend
RUNES.from: Gutanbac Shrine, the third Great Sky Island shrine.
RUNES.what: Swim straight up through any ceiling above you and pop out standing on top of it.
RUNES.tip: Use it to skip climbing in caves and towers. Aim for a flat ceiling overhead, not slopes, and watch the cursor turn yellow when it's valid.

RUNES.name: Recall
RUNES.from: From Zelda's spirit at the Temple of Time on the Great Sky Island, after clearing the first three shrines.
RUNES.what: Reverse an object's recent movement, sending it back along the exact path it just traveled.
RUNES.tip: Ride a fallen sky rock back up to the islands, or send enemy projectiles flying back. Great for reaching high places without building.

RUNES.name: Autobuild
RUNES.from: From a Construct at the Great Abandoned Central Mine in the Depths, during the quest A Mystery in the Depths (not on the Great Sky Island).
RUNES.what: Instantly recreate saved or recently built Zonai constructions, spending Zonaite for parts you don't have on hand.
RUNES.tip: Save favorite vehicles like a hover bike so you can rebuild them anywhere. Capturing schematics from machines you find expands your options.

CHAMPIONS.name: Vow of Sidon, Sage of Water
CHAMPIONS.note: Vow of Sidon, Sage of Water

ROADMAP.name: 152 Shrines
ROADMAP.note: Every shrine grants a Light of Blessing; four trade for a heart or stamina vessel. The long-haul goal across Surface and Sky.

ROADMAP.name: 120 Lightroots
ROADMAP.note: Each Lightroot mirrors a Surface shrine and lights a patch of the pitch-black Depths.

ROADMAP.name: 1000 Korok Seeds
ROADMAP.note: Tiny puzzles all over Surface, Sky, and Depths. Trade to Hestu to expand your pouches.

ROADMAP.name: Sage's Wills & armor
ROADMAP.note: Upgrade your sage abilities with Sage's Wills, and armor at the Great Fairies once you reunite Mastro's troupe.

ROADMAP.name: Sky & Depths
ROADMAP.note: Sky islands, the vast Depths, Yiga schematics, Zonai device dispensers, and the addisons — a whole game beyond the Surface.

TIPS.name: Build & Fuse freely
items: Ultrahand + Fuse is the heart of the game — fuse rocks/monster parts to weapons for power, and stick Zonai devices together to travel.
Out of battery in the sky? Recall a fallen platform and ride it back up, or glide.
Autobuild (from the Fifth Sage) recreates your favorite vehicles for a little Zonaite.

TIPS.name: Surviving the Depths
items: The Depths are pitch black and full of Gloom that caps your hearts. Carry Brightbloom Seeds (throw or fuse to arrows) and light Lightroots.
Cure gloom-damaged (cracked) hearts with Sundelion dishes or by warping to the Surface.
Every Lightroot sits directly under a Surface shrine — a handy way to find shrines.

quests.name: Today's Menu
quests.giver: Burmano (the cook, inside the Emergency Shelter at Lookout Landing)
quests.reward: Fruit and Mushroom Mix (a cooked dish)
quests.oneLine: The shelter's cook is one ingredient short of finishing his dish and asks you to bring it.
quests.how: Talk to Burmano in the Emergency Shelter and he asks for an Apple. Hand over an Apple from your materials (shake any apple tree if you have none) and he gives you a Fruit and Mushroom Mix, completing the quest.

quests.name: The Incomplete Stable
quests.giver: Karson / Lester (Hudson Construction crew at the unfinished stable just inside the south gate of Lookout Landing)
quests.reward: Builds the Lookout Landing mini-stable (board/register horses there) + 1 Pony Point
quests.oneLine: The half-built stable at Lookout Landing is missing a roof plank you can put in place.
quests.how: Available after you clear one Regional Phenomena (one temple). Grab the loose wooden plank lying near the horses with Ultrahand, rotate it to fit the gap in the stable roof and set it in place to seal the hole, then talk to Karson/Lester to finish.

quests.name: Spotting Spot
quests.giver: Lester (at the now-finished Lookout Landing mini-stable, south side)
quests.location: Lookout Landing (south) / Passeri Greenbelt just southwest
quests.reward: Purple Rupee (50), a Swift Carrot, and you keep the horse Spot
quests.oneLine: Lester wants you to catch a particular spotted horse roaming the fields nearby.
quests.how: Complete The Incomplete Stable first. Go a short way southwest to the wild horses near Passeri Greenbelt and find Spot — a beige horse with grey spots and a black mane and tail. Sneak up behind it, mount it, soothe it with the L-stick to tame it, then ride it back and register it with Lester while mounted.

quests.name: Village Attacked by Pirates
quests.giver: Garini (with Mubs, at the Lookout Landing General Store)
quests.reward: Mighty Salt-Grilled Crab
quests.oneLine: A worried merchant has heard his home village of Lurelin is being raided by pirates and wants it made safe.
quests.how: Talk to Garini or Mubs at the Lookout Landing General Store to start it. Travel to Lurelin Village in southeast Hyrule and clear out the monster pirates occupying it (the Lurelin 'Ruffian-Infested Village' liberation), then return to Garini at Lookout Landing to report the villagers are safe and collect the crab.

quests.name: A Deal With the Statue
quests.giver: The Horned Statue (in the Royal Hidden Passage, reached through the Emergency Shelter under Lookout Landing)
quests.reward: Unlocks the Horned Statue trade — sell a Heart or Stamina vessel for 100 rupees and buy either back for 120, so you can freely swap hearts for stamina (and back)
quests.oneLine: A statue offers to take a heart and let you re-spec your hearts and stamina vessels.
quests.how: Available after you clear one temple. Reach the Horned Statue at the end of the Royal Hidden Passage (the same cave as Who Goes There?). Talk to it — it takes one Heart Container and returns it on the second chat, then opens its swap-shop so you can trade hearts and stamina back and forth for rupees.

quests.name: WANTED: Stone Talus
quests.giver: Gralens (Monster-Control Crew, inside the Emergency Shelter at Lookout Landing)
quests.location: Lookout Landing (Emergency Shelter); target in Akkala
quests.reward: 100 rupees; clearing all three big-monster WANTED bounties adds 300 more rupees
quests.oneLine: Gralens posts a bounty on a large Stone Talus and marks it on your map.
quests.how: Available after you finish two 'Bring Peace to...!' raids. Talk to Gralens, choose 'How about big monsters?' then pick Stone Talus to mark it (Akkala). Ascend or climb onto the Talus, run to the ore deposit on its back, and Fuse a heavy rock or hammer to your weapon to smash the deposit until it dies. Return to Gralens for the reward.

quests.name: WANTED: Hinox
quests.giver: Gralens (Monster-Control Crew, inside the Emergency Shelter at Lookout Landing)
quests.location: Lookout Landing (Emergency Shelter); target out in the field
quests.reward: 100 rupees; clearing all three big-monster WANTED bounties adds 300 more rupees
quests.oneLine: Gralens posts a bounty on a sleeping Hinox and marks its location.
quests.how: From Gralens' 'big monsters' menu pick Hinox to mark it. Travel there and hit the Hinox in its single eye to stun it — shoot the eye with an arrow or land a strong fused blow — then pile on damage to the downed body. Repeat until it falls and report back to Gralens.

quests.name: WANTED: Molduga
quests.giver: Gralens (Monster-Control Crew, inside the Emergency Shelter at Lookout Landing)
quests.location: Lookout Landing (Emergency Shelter); target in the Gerudo Desert
quests.reward: 100 rupees; clearing all three big-monster WANTED bounties adds 300 more rupees
quests.oneLine: Gralens posts a bounty on a Molduga that swims through the desert sand.
quests.how: From Gralens' 'big monsters' menu pick Molduga to mark it (Gerudo Desert). Stand on a rock so it can't reach you, then drop or shoot a Bomb Flower (Fuse one to an arrow) onto the sand near it; the blast flips it onto its back, so strike it with strong fused melee hits. Repeat and return to Gralens.

quests.name: Unknown Sky Giant
quests.giver: Gralens (Monster-Control Crew, inside the Emergency Shelter at Lookout Landing)
quests.location: Lookout Landing (Emergency Shelter); target is a Flux Construct in the sky
quests.reward: 100 rupees; clearing all three 'Unknown' unusual-monster bounties also yields a Diamond
quests.oneLine: Gralens relays sightings of a giant block-creature in the sky and marks it for you to slay.
quests.how: After clearing all three big-monster WANTED bounties, ask Gralens about 'unusual monsters' and pick the sky giant to mark a Flux Construct (near the Wind Temple region). Pull a glowing block off it with Ultrahand to expose the core, then Ascend or climb to the core and smash it until the Construct collapses. Report to Gralens.

quests.name: Unknown Three-Headed Monster
quests.giver: Gralens (Monster-Control Crew, inside the Emergency Shelter at Lookout Landing)
quests.location: Lookout Landing (Emergency Shelter); target is a Gleeok at the Bridge of Hylia
quests.reward: 100 rupees; clearing all three 'Unknown' unusual-monster bounties also yields a Diamond
quests.oneLine: Reports of a fire-breathing three-headed dragon send Gralens' bounty to a Gleeok.
quests.how: From Gralens' 'unusual monsters' options pick the three-headed monster to mark a Flame Gleeok at the Bridge of Hylia. Shoot each of its three heads to stun them; when all heads are down the Gleeok crashes, so use a high vantage and Bullet Time (jump then draw the bow) plus fused arrows to finish it. Return to Gralens.

quests.name: Unknown Huge Silhouette
quests.giver: Gralens (Monster-Control Crew, inside the Emergency Shelter at Lookout Landing)
quests.location: Lookout Landing (Emergency Shelter); target is a Frox in the Depths
quests.reward: 100 rupees; clearing all three 'Unknown' unusual-monster bounties also yields a Diamond
quests.oneLine: Gralens marks a huge silhouette lurking below — a Frox in the Depths.
quests.how: From Gralens' 'unusual monsters' options pick the huge silhouette to mark a Frox in the Depths. Toss a bomb or Brightbloom Seed into its mouth when it tries to inhale you (or shoot the glowing bulbs on its back), then climb onto its back while it's stunned and pummel the bulbs. Repeat until it dies and report to Gralens.

quests.name: Bring Peace to Hyrule Field!
quests.giver: Hoz (Monster-Control Crew captain, on horseback in Hyrule Field just southwest of the Hyrule Field Chasm, south of Lookout Landing)
quests.location: Hyrule Field, south of Lookout Landing
quests.reward: 100 rupees; starts the next 'Bring Peace to...!' regional quest
quests.oneLine: Hoz's squad is about to storm a monster camp on Hyrule Field and asks you to join the raid.
quests.how: Find Hoz mounted near the Hyrule Field Chasm and agree to help. Ride to the nearby monster camp, talk to Hoz to begin the assault, then clear every monster (Bokoblins and a Moblin). Wiping out the camp completes it and Hoz moves on to Fort Hateno for the next region's raid.

quests.name: Hestu's Concern
quests.giver: Hestu (the giant Korok, at Lindor's Brow just northwest of Lookout Landing)
quests.location: Lindor's Brow / outskirts of Lookout Landing
quests.reward: Unlocks Hestu so you can trade Korok Seeds to expand weapon/bow/shield pouch slots
quests.oneLine: Hestu is too frightened of some nearby 'trees' to do his job until you deal with them.
quests.how: Go to Hestu at Lindor's Brow; the 'trees' he fears are Evermean monsters. Attack the suspicious trees to make them spring to life and defeat them (a few solid hits each). With them gone Hestu relaxes and will start upgrading your equipment-pouch slots in exchange for Korok Seeds. (If you've already cleared a temple, Hestu instead waits inside Lookout Landing.)

quests.name: Who Goes There?
quests.giver: Jerrin (inside the Emergency Shelter at Lookout Landing)
quests.location: Lookout Landing (Emergency Shelter / Royal Hidden Passage)
quests.reward: 20 Rupees
quests.oneLine: Jerrin heard a creepy voice through a hole in the shelter wall and wants you to check it out.
quests.how: Talk to Jerrin, then go into the Royal Hidden Passage behind the hole. Follow the tunnel and break the rocks blocking the way with a Bomb Flower or a fused hammer/heavy weapon; the voice turns out to be the Horned Statue (talking to it also begins the separate 'A Deal With the Statue'). Return to Jerrin to reassure her and finish.

quests.name: Messages from an Ancient Era
quests.giver: Wortsworth (the scholar studying a fallen tablet at Lookout Landing)
quests.location: Lookout Landing (Wortsworth later relocates to Kakariko Village)
quests.reward: 100 rupees per translated tablet, plus the Zonai Survey Team Fabric (horse caparison) on completion
quests.oneLine: A scholar wants you to photograph ancient stone tablets across Hyrule so he can translate them.
quests.how: Available after you clear a temple, when a tablet falls at Lookout Landing. Talk to Wortsworth (he then moves to Kakariko Village). Travel to each Ring Ruin / ancient tablet he marks, use the Purah Pad camera to photograph the carved text clearly, and bring the pictures back; each readable photo pays out and the full set rewards the Zonai Survey Team Fabric.

quests.name: Hateno Village Research Lab
quests.giver: Robbie (at the Lookout Landing research room) — he then has you meet him at the Hateno Ancient Tech Lab
quests.location: Lookout Landing (resolves at the Hateno Ancient Tech Lab in East Necluda)
quests.reward: Shrine Sensor (Purah Pad upgrade); opens Robbie's follow-up Purah Pad quests (Travel Medallion, Sensor +, Hero's Path)
quests.oneLine: Robbie asks you to come to his Hateno lab so he can upgrade your Purah Pad.
quests.how: Available after the main quest 'A Mystery in the Depths' and gaining Autobuild. Speak with Robbie at Lookout Landing, travel to the Hateno Ancient Tech Lab east of Hateno Village, and he installs the Shrine Sensor. Walk the lab with it until it pings the nearby hidden shrine, find that shrine, then return to Robbie to finish.

quests.name: A New Champion's Tunic
quests.giver: Reading Zelda's Diary in Zelda's Secret Well, behind Link's old house in Hateno Village
quests.location: Hateno Village (East Necluda); completed at Hyrule Castle Sanctum
quests.reward: Champion's Leathers
quests.oneLine: Zelda's diary reveals she ordered a new tunic for Link, hidden in the castle throne room.
quests.how: Read Zelda's Diary down the well behind her old Hateno house to start the quest, then go to the Sanctum (throne room) atop Hyrule Castle. Light the two torches flanking the throne (Flint, a Fire Fruit, or a fused fire item/arrow). The statue behind the throne slides aside, revealing a chest with the Champion's Leathers.

quests.name: Dantz's Prize Cows
quests.giver: Dantz, at Hateno Pasture (east edge of Hateno Village, on the path to the lab)
quests.reward: Fresh Milk (repeatable: 3 Acorns = 1 bottle)
quests.oneLine: A rancher trades you Fresh Milk for acorns to feed his cows.
quests.how: Bring Dantz 3 Acorns. Get them by chopping the trees behind the pasture or by shooting the bushy-tailed squirrels in the nearby woods, then hand them over. He gives a bottle of Fresh Milk, and you can repeat the trade any time.

quests.name: A Bottled Cry for Help
quests.giver: A message bottle washed up on Hateno Beach (southeast coast, East Necluda)
quests.location: Hateno Beach / Mapla Point Cave (East Necluda)
quests.reward: 100 Rupees
quests.oneLine: A bottled letter says a fisherman is trapped in a sea cave and needs rescuing.
quests.how: Read the bottle on the beach, then make your way to Mapla Point Cave to the southeast. Inside, use Ultrahand to pull away the boulder damming the path so Chumin can escape; talk to him for 100 Rupees.

quests.name: Manny's Beloved
quests.giver: Manny, outside the house next to Cece's shop in Hateno Village
quests.reward: Rushroom x10
quests.oneLine: Manny wants to know what Ivee, the general-store girl, likes so he can win her over.
quests.how: Talk to Manny, then ask Ivee (in front of the General Store) what she likes; she asks for 10 Hot-Footed Frogs. Catch them around water near Hateno (the well up the hill behind the store is reliable), give them to Manny, and he rewards you with 10 Rushrooms.

quests.name: Photographing a Chuchu
quests.giver: Sayge, at the Kochi Dye Shop in Hateno Village
quests.reward: Chuchu Fabric (paraglider fabric)
quests.oneLine: The dye-shop owner wants a Compendium photo of an ordinary Chuchu for paraglider inspiration.
